rho: Implementation may be slower in small builders with weak data discipline and faster in standardized production platforms.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The qualifying edge case earns primarily project and home-sale revenue under repeated relationships with external bulk or programmatic buyers. Prices are generally set per home or portfolio rather than as cost-plus labor reimbursement; warranty and handover obligations accompany the sale but are not assumed to be standalone recurring revenue.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS core is an own-account product-and-land sale rather than an outsourced service: Census defines builders as building on land they own or control and including the land in the home sale. To keep the screen coherent with the fixed recurring-or-repeat outsourced-service population, the lens is narrowed to firms with demonstrable repeat external-customer programs; ordinary one-off speculative consumer sales are excluded.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off speculative sales to individual homebuyers; land-only development; rental ownership; captive construction; non-control financing vehicles; general contracting for a customer's land; remodeling; and specialty-trade subcontracting.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market for-sale home builders with a material pattern of repeat external-customer work, principally repeated bulk or programmatic sales to institutional, investor, or community-development buyers, including the associated planning, procurement, project administration, sales coordination, handover, and warranty workflows.

caveats: The occupation source is NAICS 2361 residential building construction, not 236117 alone.
NAHB AI adoption percentages cover single-family builders and business functions, not wage-weighted task hours.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis; that vintage and basis adjustment may not reflect the narrowed repeat-buyer subset.
a.change_evidence: A 236117-specific payroll extract mapped by SOC code, combined with time-and-motion samples for estimating, scheduling, procurement, sales, warranty, finance, and site-supervision tasks and a system-level audit of AI already in production, would materially move this estimate.
a.rationale: Residential-building employment is dominated by physical trades and site supervision, while the largest occupations also include construction and general managers, project-management specialists, office clerks, bookkeepers, sales representatives, and administrative assistants. AI can substitute portions of estimating, scheduling, document control, purchasing, marketing, buyer communications, and finance work, but little direct framing, site labor, inspection, or accountable supervision. The range is a wage-weighted judgment from that mix, reduced for AI already used in marketing and market analysis.

d5.bridge: NAHB forecasts all U.S. single-family starts rising 1.0% in 2026 to 940,000 and 5% in 2027 to 984,000, while Census reported May 2026 single-family starts at an 882,000 annual rate and Freddie Mac estimated a 3.7 million-unit housing shortage in Q3 2024. I translate the near-term start outlook and supply gap into a modest cumulative real-quantity increase through year five. This can overstate the narrowed institutional-repeat-buyer segment if financing or bulk-buyer demand weakens, and understate it if housing undersupply drives sustained production. These sources cover housing units/starts, not constant-quality service-basket demand of the eligible lens.
caveats: The five-year value extrapolates beyond NAHB's explicit 2027 forecast.
Starts are broader than 236117 and broader than the narrowed repeat-buyer lens.
The Freddie Mac shortage estimate is a model-based stock gap, not realized demand or purchasing power.
d5.change_evidence: A buyer-type forecast through 2031 for build-to-rent, affordable/community-development, and other programmatic purchasers, expressed in constant-quality units and tied to financing commitments, cancellations, inventories, permits, and starts, would materially move this ratio.
d5.rationale: Underlying housing undersupply supports unit demand, but mortgage rates, credit availability, land and labor costs, local approvals, and cyclicality make a wide range necessary. The base implies only modest five-year quantity growth, not nominal price growth.

caveats: No source directly counts 236117 firms with repeat external-buyer programs.
Repeat product sales may not meet a strict interpretation of outsourced service even when contracts are programmatic.
The frozen n=1,714 is a margin-bridged estimate from firm counts and receipts using a sector margin and is itself ESTIMATE quality.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue segmentation for the 1,714 frozen LMM-band firms—buyer type, repeat-customer share, contract form, warranty/service revenue, and NAICS verification—from a commercial database plus a stratified sample of tax returns or management accounts would determine the eligible share.
e.rationale: Census defines 236117 around homes built on builder-controlled land with the land included in the sale, while construction for others belongs in 236115 or 236116. That makes ordinary 236117 activity a product/real-estate transaction, not an outsourced service. Only a small tail of LMM firms is likely to have material repeat programs with external institutional, investor, or community-development buyers that satisfy the fixed lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 236117 ($7,482,733,759) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 236117 ($248,981,827,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1414 (margins.json[236]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Homebuilding EBITDA/Sales 14.14%); revenue/employee $1,901,742 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 236117); class <5: 8,218 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,366,563 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $193,232 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,654 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,231,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $881,146 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 960 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,762,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,946,048 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 754 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,572,310 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,443,925 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 123 firms, avg revenue/firm $259,787,967 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,734,019 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,844,552,346 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $402,219,702 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,714

caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from industry structure, not directly measured.
More functions may migrate to buyer-controlled construction management or vertically integrated specialty contractors.
The estimate concerns accountable operators, not preservation of today's staffing or workflows.
o.change_evidence: Contract and operating-model evidence from large repeat buyers showing direct procurement of land, permits, trades, quality control, and warranty through software-managed self-delivery, plus the share of units delivered without a merchant-builder counterparty, would materially lower this estimate.
o.rationale: The current basket requires an accountable entity to control land, capital, design choices, permits, subcontractors, safety, quality, sales, handover, and warranty risk. Software and customer self-service can remove coordination tasks but cannot replace the party responsible for delivering a permitted physical home. NAHB's finding that the average builder subcontracts 84% of construction cost reinforces that the builder's operator role is orchestration and accountability even when trades are external.

caveats: No direct study measures five-year pass-through of AI savings in repeat-buyer homebuilding contracts.
Housing-cycle price movements, land gains, financing incentives, and mix shifts can obscure true pass-through.
This primitive excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty, which are handled elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level bid, contract, gross-margin, incentive, concession, and renewal data before and after documented workflow automation for institutional-buyer programs, with competitive win/loss records and customer interviews, would materially change the retained-benefit estimate.
q.rationale: Per-home and portfolio sale prices let a builder retain workflow savings initially because customer invoices are not direct labor-hour reimbursement. Over five years, however, sophisticated repeat buyers rebid programs, negotiate volume pricing, compare standardized products, and capture part of lower delivery cost; competitors can also convert efficiency into concessions. The discounted retained share is therefore centered at one-half rather than full retention.

caveats: Reported use of AI does not measure successful labor implementation.
The Census adoption benchmark spans all industries and firm sizes, while NAHB respondents are not limited to the frozen lens.
Implementation may be slower in small builders with weak data discipline and faster in standardized production platforms.
rho.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after operating data from at least 20 LMM production builders using AI-enabled estimating, scheduling, purchasing, document control, sales, and warranty systems, including labor hours, exception rates, rework, and realized headcount avoidance over 12 or more months, would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should progress in office-heavy workflows, but adoption is still concentrated: NAHB reported about 20% of builders using AI for advertising and marketing and 11% for market analysis and project planning in July 2025. Fragmented field data, local permitting, liability, quality control, subcontractor coordination, and human approval constrain conversion of technically exposed work into removed labor or avoided hires.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 U.S. small-business-owner survey reports that 17% of owners age 55 or older, versus 10% of younger owners, planned a sale, gift, or rare public offering in the next five years; Census data cited there put 52.3% of employer businesses under owners age 55 or older. I use 13% as an approximate blended intention rate, then widen the range. This overstates qualifying control transfers because intentions may not close and gifts/internal transfers can be nonqualifying, but it may understate unplanned sales and construction-cycle distress. The population is all U.S. small businesses, not eligible 236117 LMM firms.
caveats: Plans are not completed transfers, and the source combines sales, gifts, and rare offerings.
The survey is not construction-specific or restricted to $1–10M normalized EBITDA.
The eligible repeat-buyer subset may be more institutionalized and transferable than the small-business population.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed control transactions for private U.S. homebuilders matched to NAICS 236117, EBITDA, seller age, transaction type, and post-close operating continuity from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and lender SBA records would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: The best accessible five-year evidence measures stated transfer intentions, not completed control transactions. Builder cyclicality and land/inventory exposure can create sales, but owner dependence, guarantees, project liabilities, and poor transferability can also lead to wind-downs rather than qualifying deals.

principal_driver: AI-addressable estimating, project administration, procurement, sales, finance, and warranty coordination inside a still operator-required physical delivery model.
principal_weakness: The industry's core own-account home-and-land sale is not an outsourced recurring service, leaving only a small and poorly measured eligible subset.
